expectations.text: COMPANY.md exists at the sandbox root and contains an ownership map table with columns for Concern, Owner, and Notes
expectations.evidence: COMPANY.md at outputs/ root; lines 40-70 contain a pipe-table with header `| Concern | Owner | Notes |` and 30 substantive ownership rows (e.g., 'Brand positioning and naming | Brand & Growth | Local Beer Triangle applied').

expectations.text: COMPANY.md contains a conflict resolution table addressing boundary edges between agents
expectations.evidence: COMPANY.md lines 72-87: table with columns Boundary / Agent A / Agent B / Resolution, covering 12 concrete boundary edges (tone-of-voice doc vs execution, headline copy in hero, CTA colour, beer-specific site copy, age-gate, cookie policy text, blog SEO, booking edge cases, build vs integrate, analytics KPIs, merch SKU, stack choice).

expectations.text: Each agent has a file at .claude/agents/{lowercase_name}.md with YAML frontmatter containing name, description, tools, model, and memory fields
expectations.evidence: Path substitution applied per grader instructions: sandbox blocks .claude/ writes, files placed at outputs/agents/{brand-growth,designer,editor,engineer}.md. All four files open with YAML frontmatter containing name, description, tools, model, memory fields (verified in all four).

expectations.text: Each subagent frontmatter includes memory: project
expectations.evidence: Line 6 of each of the four agent files: `memory: project`.

expectations.text: Each subagent frontmatter description reads as a routing trigger (specifies when to invoke, mentions exclusions) — not a generic bio
expectations.evidence: Each description lists 'Use for...' domain triggers plus 'Invoke when the user asks...' phrasings, and ends with explicit exclusions. E.g. brand-growth.md: 'Not for writing long-form prose (that is the Editor), not for visual design execution (that is the Designer), not for site implementation (that is the Engineer).' Same pattern in designer, editor, engineer.

expectations.text: Each subagent body contains 6 sections: Identity, Voice, Analytical Frameworks, Boundaries, Failure Modes, Standing Instructions
expectations.evidence: All four agent files contain exactly these six H2 headings in order. Verified in brand-growth.md (lines 11, 15, 25, 37, 57, 65), designer.md (11, 15, 25, 37, 57, 65), editor.md (11, 15, 25, 37, 57, 65), engineer.md (11, 15, 25, 37, 61, 68).

expectations.text: Each subagent's Boundaries section includes Owns, Does not own, and Hands off to subsections
expectations.evidence: All four files have ### Owns / ### Does not own / ### Hands off to subheadings under Boundaries. Verified across brand-growth.md, designer.md, editor.md, engineer.md.

expectations.text: No two agents claim ownership of the same concern in the ownership map
expectations.evidence: COMPANY.md ownership map (lines 40-70): each of 30 rows has a single owner. Adjacent concerns are split cleanly (tone-of-voice *document* → Brand & Growth; editorial style guide → Editor; age-gate *policy* → Brand & Growth via conflict table; age-gate *mechanics* → Engineer). No duplicate Owner entries for the same Concern.

expectations.text: No agent-company/ folder is created in the sandbox (obsolete in v2.1)
expectations.evidence: outputs/agent-company/ folder DOES exist with subdirectories brand-growth/, designer/, editor/, engineer/, each containing calibration-responses.md. The grader path-substitution note only covers the .claude/ path rewrite and the equivalence of calibration-response location, but does not exempt the folder's existence from this assertion. v2.1 says no such folder; one was created.

expectations.text: The executor's summary/transcript shows they questioned or challenged the user's initial 3-agent suggestion rather than blindly accepting it
expectations.evidence: Executor summary: 'Kept the user's instinct but sharpened it: added compliance/positioning ownership (absent from user's three-agent proposal), split content into brewery-domain authority, and scoped web engineering to include GDPR/PECR/age-gate mechanics.' The 4-agent output (adding Brand & Growth Strategist to user's 3) reflects a substantive challenge.

expectations.text: SEO or visibility is explicitly addressed — as its own agent, or assigned in the ownership map
expectations.evidence: Ownership map line 65: 'Technical SEO and schema markup | Engineer | Structured data for beer and events'. Plus Brand & Growth owns 'Marketing plan and launch sequence' including Untappd/Google Business Profile visibility (line 34 engineer.md description; brand-growth.md frontmatter mentions 'Untappd and Google Business Profile strategy').

expectations.text: The executor's transcript shows evidence of a quality-gate checklist being applied before final delivery
expectations.evidence: Executor summary: 'All 24 calibration tests (6 per agent) simulated per the skill-substitution rule.' Each agent-company/{name}/calibration-responses.md contains 6 graded tests (domain, boundary, handoff, source-of-truth, voice-under-pressure, research-first) with PASS verdicts and explicit pass/fail criteria — functioning as the pre-delivery quality gate.

expectations.text: Each subagent Voice section includes at least 2 example sentences with specific, realistic content (not placeholder text)
expectations.evidence: Each Voice section has 3 concrete examples. E.g. designer.md: 'The booking flow is three screens... 12-18% completion per extra step — source: Baymard' and 'The logo's burgundy reads flat on beige. We need a secondary accent at 3:1 contrast minimum... Propose warm charcoal at #2A2622.' Engineer cites specific pricing (GBP 25/month Shopify Basic), LCP 2.5s thresholds. Brand & Growth names Lost and Grounded, Wiper and True. Editor gives a fully-written West Coast Pils tasting note.

expectations.text: Each subagent Analytical Frameworks section includes at least 2 named domain-specific frameworks with concrete criteria
expectations.evidence: Each file has 4 named frameworks with concrete criteria. Brand & Growth: Local Beer Triangle, Merch Gravity Test (scoring 1-5 on pull/signal/economics, cut if under 9), Portman & CAP Alcohol Screen, Launch Flywheel 90-60-30-launch. Designer: 60-30-10 Colour Discipline, Squint Test + 5-Second Clarity Test, Booking-Flow Drop-Off Diagnostic (Baymard 65-75% benchmark), Token-First Handoff Protocol. Editor: Specific-Sensory-Cultural Layering, Tone-Register Matrix, Fact-Check Triad (BJCP 2021 + recipe + named source), Eight-Word Lede Test. Engineer: Build-vs-Integrate Matrix (4 scored axes), Core Web Vitals Discipline (LCP 2.5s, CLS 0.1, INP 200ms), GDPR/PECR Checklist, Incident Response 3-Tier.

expectations.text: Each subagent Failure Modes section contains at least 3 role-specific failure modes (not generic advice)
expectations.evidence: Each file has 5 role-specific failure modes. Brand & Growth: general craft-beer-trends from training data, approving alcohol copy without running Portman/CAP, SKU proposals without margin calc, writing blog posts instead of briefs, inventing Bristol-specific facts. Designer: colours not derived from brand file, training-data design trends, handoff without tokens, writing copy, skipping a11y pass. Editor: generic adjective-stack tasting notes, inventing beer history, copy without recipe notes, conflicting-register writing, Bristol scene from training data. Engineer: defaulting to Next.js, training-data platform pricing, shipping without GDPR checklist, implementing without tokens, Lighthouse-as-proxy.

expectations.text: Each subagent Standing Instructions includes an instruction about verifying claims against current sources with URL citation
expectations.evidence: All four files contain near-identical line: 'Before giving a domain opinion or making a factual claim, verify it against current sources (web search, project files). Cite the URL or file path. Never rely solely on training data when a live source exists.' (brand-growth.md:68, designer.md:68, editor.md:68, engineer.md:71).

expectations.text: Each subagent Failure Modes includes a failure mode about relying on training data instead of researching current sources
expectations.evidence: All four files. Brand & Growth: 'Do not write brand decks based on general craft-beer trends from training data. Verify Bristol market conditions against current sources... Cite URLs.' Designer: 'Do not invent current design trends from training data... Verify against current independent-brand work on Are.na, Siteinspire, and Typewolf... Cite examples.' Editor: 'Do not rely on training data for Bristol beer scene claims...' Engineer: 'Do not rely on training data for current platform pricing, API docs, or framework versions... Verify on the vendor's current docs... Cite URLs.'

expectations.text: Each subagent Standing Instructions mentions consulting persistent memory at session start and saving learnings after significant tasks
expectations.evidence: All four files contain identical opening Standing Instructions bullet: 'Consult your persistent memory at session start (`MEMORY.md` is auto-loaded). After significant tasks, save learnings — recurring patterns, domain reference points, frameworks that worked. Curate the file; trim outdated notes.'

expectations.text: Each subagent Standing Instructions references COMPANY.md at the project root for conflict resolution
expectations.evidence: All four files: 'Conflict resolution follows `COMPANY.md` at the project root.' (brand-growth.md:70, designer.md:70, editor.md:70, engineer.md:73).

expectations.text: The ownership map in COMPANY.md is consistent with the Owns sections in each subagent file — no discrepancies
expectations.evidence: Spot-checked each Owner claim in COMPANY.md against the matching agent's ### Owns. E.g., 'Stack selection | Engineer' matches engineer.md Owns: 'Stack decisions'. 'Design tokens | Designer' matches designer.md Owns: 'design/tokens.json'. 'Age-gate mechanics | Engineer' matches engineer.md Owns: 'age-gate mechanics'. 'Portman and ASA CAP compliance sign-off | Brand & Growth' matches brand-growth.md Owns: 'Advertising-code compliance (Portman, ASA CAP) — reviews creative before it ships'. 'Fact-checking of beer claims | Editor' matches editor.md Owns: 'Fact-checking of all beer-style and brewing-process claims'. Tone-of-voice split (doc=Brand, execution-style-guide=Editor) handled consistently across both files.

claims.claim: Four agents: Brand & Growth Strategist, Visual & UX Designer, Beer Culture Editor, Web Engineer
claims.evidence: COMPANY.md lists four agents; four files exist at outputs/agents/.

claims.claim: Added compliance/positioning ownership absent from user's three-agent proposal
claims.evidence: Brand & Growth Strategist is a net-new agent not in the user's (design/content/web) triplet. Owns Portman/ASA CAP compliance and positioning.

claims.claim: All 24 calibration tests (6 per agent) simulated and passed without iterations
claims.evidence: Each calibration-responses.md has 6 tests with 'Grade: PASS' on every question. Summary lines: 'All 6 pass. No iterations required' / 'All 6 pass' / 'All 6 pass. Voice held...' / 'All 6 pass.'

Engineer file has GDPR/PECR Compliance Checklist framework naming ICO guidance, UK-specific cookie consent requirements. Brand & Growth names Portman Group and ASA CAP codes (UK alcohol regulators). Engineer calibration Q6 checks UK Shopify pricing with VAT. Age-gate tied to Challenge 25 / Licensing Act 2003 Section 146-147 (brand-growth calibration Q3).

claims.claim: No two agents overlap in ownership
claims.evidence: Ownership map has 30 distinct concerns each with one owner. Adjacent/risky areas (tone doc vs execution style, age-gate policy vs mechanics, KPI definition vs instrumentation) are split explicitly in the Conflict Resolution table.

claims.claim: Sandbox blocked writes to .claude/ paths — files live under agents/ instead
claims.evidence: Files exist at outputs/agents/ not outputs/.claude/agents/. COMPANY.md line 6 documents the sandbox constraint and the deployment path substitution.

workarounds: Sandbox blocked writes to .claude/ paths — subagent files placed under agents/ instead. COMPANY.md documents the deployment path substitution.
agent-company/ folder used as workspace for calibration-responses despite v2.1 deprecating it for runtime.

suggestions.assertion: No agent-company/ folder is created in the sandbox (obsolete in v2.1)
suggestions.reason: Ambiguous with the skill's calibration workflow. If calibration-responses are the designated quality-gate artefact, they need somewhere to live. Either (a) move calibration-responses into the workspace folder (outside outputs/), or (b) rename the assertion to 'no persistent runtime agent-company/ folder with ongoing state files (MEMORY.md, inbox/, etc.)'. As written it penalises the skill for a harness-visible test artefact.

suggestions.assertion: The executor's transcript shows evidence of a quality-gate checklist being applied before final delivery
suggestions.reason: Currently passes via the calibration PASS grades in each responses file, but there is no separate 'checklist' artefact — it is implicit in the calibration structure. Consider requiring a named QA-gate file (e.g., QUALITY-GATE.md listing: ownership map consistency, persona calibration pass, boundary-edge coverage, research-first instruction presence) so the assertion is discriminating.

No assertion checks that frontmatter descriptions actually differentiate routing — two agents could have nearly-identical descriptions and still pass assertion 5. Consider asserting that each description's domain keywords are disjoint from the other three agents'.

No assertion checks that the Analytical Frameworks are genuinely domain-specific (not repackaged generic strategy). The rubric 'named + concrete criteria' is met here, but a weaker run could satisfy it with RICE/SWOT-style imports. Consider asserting at least one framework per agent names a domain-specific source (BJCP, Portman Group, WCAG, Baymard, ICO) as grounding.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions largely test genuine substance. Two areas deserve tightening: (1) the 'no agent-company/ folder' rule collides with the skill's own calibration workflow — disambiguate runtime vs test artefacts; (2) the quality-gate assertion is soft without a named checklist artefact. Overall this evaluation demonstrated the skill produced a calibrated, internally-consistent 4-agent company with genuine domain grounding, not surface compliance.
